audit_status.source_base: Hebrew Joshua / Yehoshua Joseph/Ephraim boundary and Gezer forced-labor register, draft aligned later
audit_status.review_note: First-pass draft reviewed for Joseph's lot, Bethel/Luz geography, Beth-horon and Gezer border, Ephraim territory, enclave cities within Manasseh, continuing Canaanite presence, and forced-labor subordination.

verses.restored: Lot for sons of Joseph went out from Jordan at Jericho, east of waters of Jericho, toward wilderness going up from Jericho through hill country to Bethel.
It went out from Bethel to Luz and passed to border of Archites at Ataroth.
It went down westward to border of Japhletites as far as border of Lower Beth-horon and as far as Gezer, and its end was sea.
Sons of Joseph, Manasseh and Ephraim, received their inheritance.
Border of sons of Ephraim according to their clans was this: border of their inheritance eastward was Ataroth-addar as far as Upper Beth-horon.
Border went out toward sea; Michmethath was on north, and border turned eastward to Taanath-shiloh and passed it eastward to Janoah.
It went down from Janoah to Ataroth and Naarah, touched Jericho, and went out at Jordan.
From Tappuah border went westward to wadi Kanah, and its end was sea. This is inheritance of tribe of sons of Ephraim according to their clans,
together with cities separated for sons of Ephraim in midst of inheritance of sons of Manasseh, all cities and their villages.
But they did not dispossess Canaanite dwelling in Gezer; Canaanite dwells in midst of Ephraim to this day and became forced labor serving under tribute.

translation_notes: Joseph's inheritance is divided between Ephraim and Manasseh, continuing the double-tribe structure established in Torah.
Bethel and Luz appear in close relation but their exact naming and site history require textual and archaeological audit.
The chapter's border depends on ancient settlements and waterways whose identifications remain debated.
Ephraim receives enclave cities inside Manasseh's territory, showing that tribal maps are not perfectly contiguous modern administrative borders.
The final verse again contradicts total-dispossession rhetoric: Canaanites remain in Gezer.
Their survival is converted into forced labor and tribute rather than equal coexistence. Continued presence alone should not be romanticized when structured by coercion.
Assigned boundaries and tributary control cannot serve as modern land claims or models for governing minority populations.
safety_note: Do not use Joshua 16 to justify modern borders, ethnic domination, forced labor, tribute systems, segregation, or exploitation of surviving populations. Center the text's admission that maps are porous, inhabitants remain, and survival under coerced labor is not justice.
